transcripts.prose:
I'll work through this step by step. Let me start by loading the croup guideline.The presentation matches the moderate severity row cleanly (inspiratory stridor at rest, mild suprasternal/intercostal recession, alert and mildly distressed). Differential check is clean against the note — no toxic appearance, no drooling, no choking history, no allergen exposure, URTI prodrome is typical. Weight is documented.Assessment: Classic moderate viral croup — inspiratory stridor at rest with mild suprasternal/intercostal recession, alert and well-perfused, oxygen saturation preserved. The history (URTI prodrome, gradual onset, low-grade fever, barky cough) and the absence of toxic features, drooling, choking history, or allergen exposure rule the must-not-miss differentials (epiglottitis, bacterial tracheitis, foreign body, anaphylaxis) clinically unlikely. Severity is **moderate**.

Plan: Single oral dose of dexamethasone as per Starship guideline. Settle Jack with parent, minimise distress (no unnecessary examinations or cannulation — agitation worsens stridor), and observe in a monitored area.

Reassessment: Watch the trajectory of stridor at rest and work of breathing — improvement is expected over the next couple of hours as the steroid takes effect. Any escalation in respiratory effort, change in mental state, or drop in saturations should prompt re-treatment as severe and senior review. Note the universal rail that moderate croup is not discharged at night regardless of trajectory.

severity_rows.label: mild
severity_rows.description: stridor only on exertion; no rest stridor; minimal recession; alert and calm

severity_rows.label: moderate
severity_rows.description: inspiratory stridor at rest; mild-to-moderate suprasternal or intercostal recession; alert, mildly distressed

severity_rows.label: severe
severity_rows.description: persistent stridor; severe recession with accessory muscle use; agitation trending to lethargy; hypoxia

severity_rows.label: impending_respiratory_failure
severity_rows.description: stridor may diminish (ominous); exhausted; obtunded; severe hypoxia or cyanosis

distinguishing_features: muffled voice rather than barky cough
sudden onset stridor without preceding URTI

transcripts.prose:
I'll work through this systematically — load the AU croup guideline, then calculate dose and reassessment plan.Differential check is clean — Jack has a clear viral URTI-pattern croup picture (barky cough, hoarseness, rest stridor, mild recession, low-grade fever, alert). No drooling, no toxic appearance, no choking history, no asymmetric findings, no allergen exposure. Severity row matches **moderate** (inspiratory stridor at rest, mild recession, alert and mildly distressed).**Assessment:** 3-year-old with a classic moderate viral croup picture — barky cough, hoarseness, rest stridor, mild recession, alert and mildly distressed, well-oxygenated. Differential against epiglottitis, bacterial tracheitis, foreign body, and anaphylaxis is clean.

**Plan:** Single oral dose of dexamethasone, given with the child kept calm on the parent's lap to avoid distress-driven worsening. Observe in the department through the period of expected onset; the dose card and reassessment card below carry the validated specifics.

**Reassessment:** Watch the trajectory of rest stridor and work of breathing, the SpO2, and whether he is feeding and settled. Worsening recession, agitation, or any drop in saturations means re-route to the severe pathway with nebulised adrenaline and early anaesthetics/PICU involvement — do not wait for the scheduled check if the picture changes.

transcripts.prose:
I'll work through this systematically. Severe croup with hypoxia needs prompt action, but the picture is classic (URTI prodrome, stridor, recession) and the must-not-miss differentials are explicitly negative (no drooling, no tripod, no toxic appearance) — so this is croup, not epiglottitis or foreign body.Severity row "severe" matches cleanly: persistent stridor, severe recession, agitation, hypoxia. Dose rule is `croup-dex-severe`.Assessment: Sione has severe croup — rest stridor, severe recession, agitation and hypoxia all fit the severe row, and the must-not-miss differentials (epiglottitis, bacterial tracheitis, foreign body, anaphylaxis) are explicitly negative on the note. Severity is severe.

Plan: Give oral dexamethasone now as charted. Alongside the steroid, this child needs concurrent severe-croup supportive care — minimise distress, supplemental oxygen for the SpO2 of 91%, and nebulised adrenaline should be considered immediately given the rest stridor with hypoxia and agitation. Manage in a resus-capable area, not a side room.

Reassessment: Watch the trajectory of work of breathing, oxygenation, and conscious state, and track response to any adrenaline given (sustained vs rebound). A rebound picture or poor response should raise bacterial tracheitis as a differential even though the initial picture fits croup. The universal rail bears repeating — if this child deteriorates to needing intubation, IV access attempts can precipitate arrest; senior airway support and gas induction is the safer path.

transcripts.prose:
I'll start by loading the croup guideline to see the severity definitions and dose rules.Reading the severity rows: mild is "stridor only on exertion; no rest stridor; minimal recession." Tane had intermittent stridor on walk-in but it settled at rest, with recession only during cough episodes. That matches the mild description — stridor is not present at rest. Both mild and moderate map to the same dose rule (`croup-dex-moderate`), so the dose selection is unambiguous. Differential check is clean — no drooling, no toxic appearance, no sudden-onset/choking history, URTI-pattern barky cough.Assessment: Tane has a classic croup picture — barky cough, hoarseness, intermittent stridor that settled with calm positioning, recession only during cough episodes, and normal saturations at rest. By the Starship severity descriptions this is **mild** (stridor on exertion but not at rest, minimal recession, alert and calm). Mild and moderate share the same dexamethasone rule, so the dose is the same either way — the distinction matters more for disposition than for dosing. Differential is reassuring: no drooling, no toxic appearance, no choking history, URTI-pattern presentation.

Plan: single oral dose of dexamethasone as per the card. Keep him calm with the parent — distress drives stridor and recession in croup, and you've already seen that play out. Observe rather than discharge straight away so you can confirm the trajectory.

Reassessment: watch for stridor returning at rest, increasing work of breathing, any drop in saturations, or change in mental state from settled-alert toward agitated or drowsy. The reassessment card carries the window and the branch points.

The stated assessment ("severe croup") does not match Mia's examination. Per the loaded Starship guideline, severe croup requires persistent stridor, severe recession with accessory muscle use, agitation trending to lethargy, or hypoxia. Mia is alert, has no stridor at rest (only with crying), no recession, SpO2 99% on room air, and is tolerating oral fluids — that picture fits the **mild** row of the severity table (stridor only on exertion, no rest stridor, minimal recession, alert and calm).

The two severity rows map to very different doses, so I do not want to proceed on the label alone when the examination points elsewhere.

Could you confirm the intended severity for dosing — is this mild croup, or are there findings not captured in the note that support severe?Waiting for your confirmation on severity before proceeding to dose calculation.

transcripts.prose:
I cannot compute this dose: airway_emergency.

The presentation has hard signs of an upper-airway emergency — drooling, tripod posturing, muffled voice, toxic appearance, and stridor with hypoxia on room air — consistent with epiglottitis, a must-not-miss alternative on the croup differential. Steroid dosing is not the right next action and would delay definitive management.

Escalate immediately per local airway-emergency protocol: call senior anaesthetics and ENT now, keep the child with the parent in a position of comfort, do not attempt airway examination, IV cannulation, or any distressing intervention, give high-flow oxygen as tolerated, and prepare for inhalational induction in a controlled setting (theatre or resus) with a surgical airway backup. Empirical IV antibiotics and steroids can be given *after* the airway is secured, not before.

I'll check the guideline registry for asthma to confirm scope.I cannot compute this dose: out_of_scope.
This skill currently supports paediatric croup only — asthma is not in the modelled guideline set.
Please use your local paediatric asthma guideline or the appropriate guideline-backed tool for salbutamol / steroid dosing in acute asthma.

output.message: No guideline modelled for "asthma" in NZ. v3.1 covers croup only — other conditions are deferred.

Once the weight is confirmed I'll calculate and pull the reassessment plan.

transcripts.prose:
I'll work through this carefully. A 17-year-old with new-onset barky cough and stridor is an atypical presentation for croup — croup is overwhelmingly a 6-month to 6-year-old condition. Let me pull the guideline to check the differential.Looking at the differential check against this presentation: a 17-year-old with a 4-hour history of stridor and **no URTI prodrome** is a strong mismatch for croup, which is typically a young child's viral illness with a preceding coryzal phase. The note's "no URTI prodrome" plus the age maps onto two must-not-miss alternatives on the guideline's differential simultaneously — bacterial tracheitis (rapid stridor onset, no prodrome) and foreign body aspiration (sudden stridor without preceding URTI). Epiglottitis also remains live in this age group, even though Marcus isn't currently toxic-appearing.

He is clinically stable right now (SpO2 96%, alert, RR 22, only mild recession), so this is not an active airway emergency requiring immediate escalation without clarification — but the diagnosis is genuinely ambiguous, and a steroid dose anchored to "croup" in a 17-year-old without a prodrome is the wrong next action.

---

I cannot compute this dose: unresolved_dangers.

The presentation is atypical for croup — adolescent age, no URTI prodrome, acute stridor onset — and fits two or more must-not-miss alternatives on the croup differential (bacterial tracheitis, foreign body aspiration, with epiglottitis not excluded). The patient is currently stable, so this is a diagnostic question, not an escalation.

Please confirm the working diagnosis before steroid dosing — consider lateral neck imaging, ENT/anaesthetic input, and a focused history for choking episode or preceding sore throat with rapid progression. If the picture clarifies as croup, I can dose dexamethasone on the next turn.

transcripts.prose: I have three different weights on file (14.2 kg at triage, 16.4 kg in cubicle, 15 kg at the GP last week) and they disagree by enough to materially change the dose. I need to resolve this before calculating.Waiting on your confirmation of the weight to use before I proceed.

transcripts.prose:
I'll pull the reassessment plan from the same guideline that backed the dose.Assessment: Jack had moderate croup and has received his single oral dexamethasone dose — onset of clinical effect is typically within an hour or two, with peak effect around the reassessment window. The reassessment card below sets the timing and the branch logic.

Plan: Keep him in a calm environment with a parent — agitation worsens stridor, so minimise interventions. He should stay under observation through the reassessment window; the card flags the trajectory-based branches (discharge, extended observation, or step-up to nebulised adrenaline).

Watching for: the trend matters more than any single sign — is the stridor at rest settling, is his work of breathing easing, is he staying alert and engaged? The reassessment card lists the specific deterioration signs that should trigger escalation, and note the standing rail that moderate croup is not discharged overnight regardless of how well he looks.

transcripts.prose:
I'll recalculate with the corrected weight of 16 kg.I'm carrying forward the moderate severity from the prior assessment — let me know if Jack's clinical picture has shifted since then and I'll re-grade.Assessment: weight corrected to 16 kg; severity carried forward as moderate from the earlier review. Dose recalculated and reassessment plan refreshed against the same Starship guideline.

Plan: oral dexamethasone, single dose. The dose card and reassessment card above reflect the corrected weight — please confirm before administering, and update the chart so the earlier 14.2 kg entry is superseded.

Reassessment: watch the trajectory of work of breathing and stridor at rest, and re-grade if there's any agitation or drop in saturations. Don't discharge moderate croup overnight regardless of how well he looks at the reassessment window.
